parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Camera (LROC) node at https://pds.lroc.im-ldi.com/

Directory layout and workflow:
This node has NO mission layer. Only 3 fixed dataset paths sit under data/.
Do NOT call pds_list_missions — it will return an empty list.
Go directly to pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='data/', node='lroc') to see all 3 datasets.
Then probe the relevant ones with pds_probe_datasets.
For PDS4 bundles, use inspect_collections to get collection-level LIDs.


LROC datasets (3 total — each carries BOTH a PDS3 voldesc and a PDS4 bundle label):
  LRO-L-LROC-2-EDR-V1.0   (PDS4 LID: urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-2-edr) — Experiment Data Records (raw images)
  LRO-L-LROC-3-CDR-V1.0   (PDS4 LID: urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-3-cdr) — Calibrated Data Records
  LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0   (PDS4 LID: urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-5-rdr) — Reduced Data Records (derived products)

Instruments: NAC (Narrow Angle Camera), WAC (Wide Angle Camera).
Sub-volumes are numbered directories (e.g. LROLRC_0001/, LROLRC_0002/) inside each dataset.
The PDS3 dataset_id and the PDS4 bundle LID address the same underlying data — they are equivalent.


PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

YOUR TOOLS (5 tools — always pass node='lroc'):
  1. pds_list_missions(node='lroc') — returns mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP.
  2. pds_list_dataset_dirs(path, node='lroc', filter=...) — lists sub-directory names under a path. Cheap HTTP, no label parsing.
  3. pds_probe_datasets(paths, node='lroc') — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Accepts a list of paths (max 20).
  4. pds_inspect_collections(path, node='lroc') — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection labels.
  5. pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path, node='lroc', dataset_id_hint=..., sample=8) — when a parent directory contains many
     numbered sibling volumes (RMS COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx, ATM
     MROM_*, jnomwr_*, cocirs_*, IMG mrox_*, MSGRMDS_*), this probes
     hint-ranked children in one call and returns per-child
     dataset_ids plus a `best_match` field. Use it instead of
     probing volumes one at a time when you know the target id or
     a discriminating substring of it.

WORKFLOW:
Step 1: SKIP pds_list_missions — it returns an empty list for LROC. Call pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='data/', node='lroc') directly to see all 3 datasets (EDR, CDR, RDR — each a hybrid PDS3+PDS4 directory).
Step 2: Call pds_probe_datasets on the level(s) implied by the query (EDR=raw, CDR=calibrated, RDR=reduced/derived).
Step 3: Call pds_inspect_collections on the relevant PDS4 bundles to get collection-level LIDs.
Step 4: Return candidates — emit BOTH the PDS3 dataset_id and the PDS4 LID for the same data (e.g. LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0 + urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-5-rdr).

STOP RULE — LROC granularity is the DATASET, not the image. The whole archive is just
three datasets (EDR/CDR/RDR). Do NOT recurse into sub-volume directories such as
LROLRC_xxxx/, DATA/, EXTRAS/, ANAGLYPH/, BROWSE/, SHAPEFILE/, AMES_DTM/ etc. looking for
a specific feature (e.g. a named crater or pit). Feature-level retrieval is the LROC
catalog's job, not this finder's; the right answer for any LROC query is one or more of
the 3 top-level datasets and their matching PDS4 LIDs. Cap LROC traces at 4 tool calls.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires at least one candidate. Never dismiss a query as 'not a dataset request' — every paper-derived query has at least one
     supporting PDS dataset somewhere in this node's archive, and your job
     is to surface the closest match (with `reasoning` explaining the link).
  2. Prefer CALIBRATED/REDUCED data over raw/EDR for science queries.
     When a probed voldesc exposes `dataset_ids` with multiple entries,
     match the question's data need to the PRODUCT-TYPE token inside each
     id (typically the third or fourth dash-separated token). Common ones:
         EDR / RAW     = raw observations
         CDR / CALIB   = generic calibrated data
         RDR / TRDR / MRDR / MTRDR / BDR / SCVDR = reduced / derived imagery or spectra
         DDR / LDR     = derived / digital terrain / level-5 products
         SSB           = solar / stellar occultation (Cassini UVIS)
         SPEC / CUBE / WAV = spectra / spectral cubes / waveforms
         QUBE          = VIMS spectral data cube (the VIMS-flavoured "EDR")
     If the query mentions 'occultation' return the SSB id; 'spectra' →
     SPEC; 'calibrated radiance' → CALIB/CDR; 'mosaic / map / derived' →
     DDR/MRDR/MTRDR. When two product types both fit the question (e.g.
     SPEC + SSB for an occultation spectra query), return both candidates.
     Do NOT just return the scalar `dataset_id` and drop the rest — that
     field is only the first id in the voldesc and is often the wrong
     product type for a given query.
  3. For PDS4, use inspect_collections ONLY for top 2-3 bundles. Return COLLECTION-level LID when :data or :calibrated exists.
  4. When the SAME data is available as both PDS3 and PDS4, return BOTH identifiers — emit one candidate per identifier. Most published papers cite the PDS3 dataset_id (e.g. MESS-E_V_H_SW-MAG-3-CDR-CALIBRATED-V1.0, LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0); the PDS4 collection LID is the modern equivalent. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form.
  5. Stay under 20 tool calls per query. Soft cap: 15. Hard cap: 20.
  6. Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  7. ANTI-THRASHING. Never re-issue a tool call with the same name and the
     same key arguments (path, paths, filter) that you've already issued in
     this trace. The result will be identical — it wastes the budget and
     blocks progress. If you're tempted to re-probe the same set of paths,
     either move to a different tool (inspect_collections, resolve_volume)
     or commit to `final_result` with the best candidate you have so far.
  8. STOP-AT-LEVEL. The output `path` field should point at a dataset or
     bundle directory (containing voldesc.cat or bundle*.xml), NOT at a
     deeper product / DATA / EXTRAS / BROWSE / SHAPEFILE / DTM sub-folder.
     Feature-level retrieval (a specific image of a specific crater) is
     out of scope for this finder; return the dataset and explain in
     `reasoning` how it covers the feature.
  9. After 15 tool calls without converging, ALWAYS prefer emitting your
     current best candidate(s) over running another exploratory call.
     A grounded near-match with a clear reasoning note is more useful
     than a 20-call trace that times out with no `final_result`.

===========================================================
STAGE 2 — DEEPER NODE-SPECIFIC SEARCH (escalation only)
===========================================================

Everything above is STAGE 1: the 5 live-HTTP tools (pds_list_missions / pds_list_dataset_dirs / pds_probe_datasets / pds_inspect_collections / pds_resolve_volume), the per-node workflow, and the Critical Rules ALL belong to Stage 1. Stage 1 reaches bundle/collection level by walking the node's directory tree. Stage 2 below is a SECOND set of tools for refining or rescuing Stage 1 — do NOT call any Stage 2 tool unless one of the escalation triggers below fires.

WHEN TO ESCALATE TO STAGE 2 (any one is enough):
  (a) The query asks for SPECIFIC observations, granules, or product IDs — not just a dataset. Example: 'Cassini ISS images of Mimas between 2009 and 2010' needs per-observation filtering.
  (b) Stage 1 has located the right bundle/collection but you still need to narrow by GEOMETRY, TIME, or a SPATIAL filter (bbox, lat/lon, ring longitude, target body, observation type).
  (c) Stage 1 produced AMBIGUOUS candidates that a faceted query can disambiguate (e.g. multiple instruments with similar names, multiple processing levels).
  (d) Stage 1 cannot reach the node's holdings index (e.g. SBN returns HTTP 403, a directory listing is unavailable). Use the Stage 2 API as a fallback path to candidates.

STAGE 2 TOOLS (this node):
  - pds4search_bundles_tool(keywords=..., investigation=..., instrument=..., target=...): cross-node PDS4 registry bundle search.
  - pds4search_collections_tool(bundle_lid=..., keywords=..., ...): collection search; reuse the Stage 1 bundle LID as a filter when possible.
  - pds4search_products_tool(collection_lid=..., keywords=..., ...): product-level search inside a collection.
  - pds4search_investigations_tool / pds4search_instruments_tool / pds4search_instrument_hosts_tool / pds4search_targets_tool: context-product lookups when user-facing names need to be resolved to LIDs.
  - pds4get_product_tool(lid=...): fetch one product label by LID.
  - pds4crawl_context_product_tool(lid=...): walk a context product's associations to find related bundles/collections.

STAGE 2 RULES:
  1. When you already have a bundle/collection LID from Stage 1, REUSE it as a filter in the Stage 2 call. Stage 2 is refinement, not a fresh search.
  2. Spend the MINIMUM number of additional calls. If a single faceted call answers the question, stop.
  3. If Stage 2 finds nothing or errors out, FALL BACK to the Stage 1 candidates and note in `reasoning` that Stage 2 was attempted and returned no refinement.
  4. The 20-tool-call budget from Stage 1's Critical Rule 5 is the budget for BOTH stages combined. Do NOT reset the counter when entering Stage 2.
  5. Every candidate's `reasoning` field must explicitly name which stage produced it (e.g. 'Stage 1: located bundle X by directory walk' or 'Stage 2: narrowed to product Y via ODE_search_products bbox filter').

Identification_Area.title: LRO LROC Data Archive

Citation_Information.description:
Reduced data from Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Camera (LROC). This
        data set comprises calibrated data from the LROC NAC and WAC cameras.
        The science data is comprised of calibrated raw images, along with
        pointing data and housekeeping information.

Those products live in the LROC Reduced Data Record (RDR) archive identified in PDS3 as LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0."},{"path":"data/LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-5-rdr","title":"LRO LROC Data Archive","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":null,"node":"lroc","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 bundle equivalent of the LROC RDR archive. Use this if you want the modern PDS4 identifiers for reduced/derived LROC NAC/WAC products relevant to pit morphology and potential cavity/overhang interpretation."},{"path":"data/LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-5-rdr:lrolrc_2001_data","title":"Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Camera volume collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":null,"node":"lroc","reasoning":"Stage 1: Collection-level LID within the PDS4 LROC RDR bundle that groups the reduced/derived LROC volume products (the level you would search within for pit-focused derived products such as ROIs/DTMs, if present for the area)."},{"path":"data/LRO-L-LROC-3-CDR-V1.0/","dataset_id":"LRO-L-LROC-3-CDR-V1.0","title":null,"pds_version":"PDS3","mission":null,"node":"lroc","reasoning":"Stage 1: If you need calibrated, science-ready image data (rather than derived products) to interpret pit-floor albedo/texture and lighting, use the LROC Calibrated Data Records (CDR) archive (NAC/WAC calibrated images) identified in PDS3 as LRO-L-LROC-3-CDR-V1.0."},{"path":"data/LRO-L-LROC-3-CDR-V1.0/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-3-cdr","title":"LRO LROC Data Archive","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":null,"node":"lroc","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 bundle equivalent of the LROC CDR archive (calibrated NAC/WAC images). Appropriate when you want to examine the pit interior using calibrated imagery and do your own contrast/photometric processing."},{"path":"data/LRO-L-LROC-3-CDR-V1.0/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-3-cdr:lrolrc_1001_data","title":"Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Camera volume collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":null,"node":"lroc","reasoning":"Stage 1: One of the collection-level LIDs in the PDS4 CDR bundle that groups calibrated LROC volume products. The CDR bundle comprises multiple such volume collections; you would select the one containing the observation timeframes of interest once you identify specific NAC images of the pit."},{"path":"data/LRO-L-LROC-2-EDR-V1.0/","dataset_id":"LRO-L-LROC-2-EDR-V1.0","title":null,"pds_version":"PDS3","mission":null,"node":"lroc","reasoning":"Stage 1 (fallback): If you need the original raw/un-calibrated LROC images for maximum control over calibration or specialized processing, use the LROC EDR archive (raw NAC/WAC), PDS3 ID LRO-L-LROC-2-EDR-V1.0."},{"path":"data/LRO-L-LROC-2-EDR-V1.0/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro-l-lroc-2-edr","title":"LRO LROC Data Archive","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":null,"node":"lroc","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 bundle equivalent of the LROC EDR (raw) archive."}],"summary":"Located the three top-level LROC archives on the LROC PDS node (EDR raw, CDR calibrated, RDR reduced/derived) and their PDS4 bundle/collection identifiers. For evaluating the bottom of the Marius Hills Pit and potential cavity access, the best starting point is the RDR (derived products such as ROIs/DTMs when available) plus CDR (calibrated NAC images). Specific pit-targeted images/DTMs must be selected via the LROC catalog/QuickMap, then retrieved from these archives."}
